context.text: Magellan was killed in the Battle of Mactan in the Philippines on April 27, 1521.

context.title: Philippines
context.text: The Philippines is an archipelago nation, not a single island. Its population as of 2023 is approximately 115 million.

question: What is the altitude of the capital of the country where the Nazca Lines are located?

context.title: Nazca Lines
context.text: The Nazca Lines are a series of ancient geoglyphs in southern Peru.

context.title: Peru
context.text: The capital of Peru is Lima.

context.title: Lima
context.text: Lima is situated on the coast of Peru at an altitude of approximately 154 meters above sea level.

question: How many Oscar nominations did the film directed by the person who also directed the movie about the sinking ship that won 11 Oscars receive for their sci-fi sequel?

context.title: Oscar records
context.text: Titanic won 11 Academy Awards and was directed by James Cameron.

context.title: James Cameron
context.text: James Cameron directed Titanic, Avatar, and Aliens. Avatar received 9 Oscar nominations.

context.title: Avatar sequels
context.text: Avatar: The Way of Water received 4 Oscar nominations. The question is ambiguous about which sci-fi sequel is being asked about.

question: What is the half-life of the isotope used in the power source of the rover exploring the crater named after a scientist on Mars?

context.title: Mars rovers
context.text: NASA's Curiosity rover has been exploring Gale Crater on Mars since 2012.

context.title: Gale Crater
context.text: Gale Crater is named after Australian astronomer Walter Frederick Gale.

context.title: Curiosity power
context.text: Curiosity is powered by a radioisotope thermoelectric generator using plutonium-238.

context.title: Plutonium-238
context.text: Plutonium-238 has a half-life of 87.7 years.

question: What was the original purpose of the tower in the city that hosted the 1900 World's Fair that a famous French engineer built?
gold_answer: entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair

context.title: 1900 World's Fair
context.text: The 1900 World's Fair was held in Paris, France.

context.title: Paris landmarks
context.text: The Eiffel Tower is the most famous landmark in Paris, built by engineer Gustave Eiffel.

context.title: Eiffel Tower
context.text: The Eiffel Tower was originally built as the entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair, not the 1900 one.

question: How many moons does the planet named after the Roman god of the sea that was discovered by mathematical prediction have?

context.title: Roman gods planets
context.text: Neptune is named after the Roman god of the sea.

context.title: Neptune discovery
context.text: Neptune was the first planet discovered by mathematical prediction rather than direct observation, in 1846.

context.title: Neptune
context.text: Neptune has 16 known moons, the largest of which is Triton.

question: What is the depth of the trench in the ocean where the ship that hit an iceberg on its maiden voyage sank?

context.title: Iceberg sinking
context.text: The RMS Titanic struck an iceberg and sank on April 15, 1912, during its maiden voyage.

context.title: Titanic location
context.text: The Titanic sank in the North Atlantic Ocean at a depth of about 3,800 meters.

context.title: Atlantic trenches
context.text: The North Atlantic does not contain a major oceanic trench. The Puerto Rico Trench is in the western Atlantic at 8,376 meters deep, but the Titanic did not sink in a trench.

question: What was the reign length of the longest-ruling monarch of the country that colonized the nation where apartheid was practiced?

context.title: Apartheid
context.text: Apartheid was a system of racial segregation in South Africa.

context.title: South Africa colonization
context.text: South Africa was colonized by both the Dutch and the British. It was a British colony until 1961.

context.title: British monarchs
context.text: Queen Victoria was the longest-reigning British monarch before Queen Elizabeth II, who reigned for 70 years.

context.title: Queen Victoria
context.text: Queen Victoria reigned for 63 years from 1837 to 1901, during which much of the British colonization of South Africa occurred.

question: What is the frequency of the note that the orchestra tunes to, which is named after a letter in the alphabet used by the country where Stradivarius violins were made?

context.title: Stradivarius
context.text: Stradivarius violins were made by Antonio Stradivari in Cremona, Italy.

context.title: Italy
context.text: Italy uses the Latin alphabet.

context.title: Orchestra tuning
context.text: Orchestras tune to the note A above middle C, which is standardized at 440 Hz.

question: How many bones are in the body part that the sport invented by James Naismith is named after?

context.title: James Naismith
context.text: James Naismith invented basketball in 1891.

context.title: Basketball
context.text: Basketball is named after the basket used as the original goal, not after a body part. The ball is shot into a basket mounted on a pole.

question: What is the average depth of the body of water that the canal built by Ferdinand de Lesseps connects to the Indian Ocean?

context.title: Ferdinand de Lesseps
context.text: Ferdinand de Lesseps oversaw the construction of the Suez Canal, which opened in 1869.

context.title: Suez Canal
context.text: The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.

context.title: Red Sea
context.text: The Red Sea connects to the Indian Ocean and has an average depth of approximately 490 meters.

context.title: Mediterranean Sea
context.text: The Mediterranean Sea has an average depth of approximately 1,500 meters. The question is ambiguous about which body of water.
